 no pneumothorax.  The cardiac silhouette remains mild to moderately enlarged. 
 Mediastinal contours are stable.

 has been interval placement of an endotracheal tube, terminating approximately

 base opacity may be a combination of pleural effusion and atelectasis,

 stable.  Left humeral head is not seen, although not dedicated imaging of the

impression_text:
1.  Endotracheal tube has its tip at the thoracic inlet.  There is a
 nasogastric tube coursing below the diaphragm with the tip not identified. 
 Left internal jugular central line has its tip in the proximal SVC.

 patchy opacities within both lungs which may represent areas of edema,
 pulmonary hemorrhage, aspiration or pneumonia.  Clinical correlation is
 advised.  No pneumothorax is appreciated.  There is likely a small left
 pleural effusion. Bones are osteopenic.  There is narrowing of the right
 acromiohumeral distance which likely reflects chronic rotator cuff tear as
 well as superimposed degenerative changes.  The left humeral head has either
 been resected or has resorbed due to remote trauma.

impression_text:
1.  Endotracheal tube continues to have its tip approximately 3 cm above the
 carina.  The nasogastric tube now has its tip projecting over the stomach.

 is status post median sternotomy with stable cardiac enlargement and stable
 mediastinal contours.  There continues to be retrocardiac consolidation, which
 may reflect partial lower lobe atelectasis given the associated layering
 pleural effusion.  There is a slight interval improvement in the bilateral

 setting of asymmetric pulmonary edema.  Clinical correlation is advised.

 is destruction or loss of the left humeral head, possibly related to remote
 trauma with resorption or previous surgical resection.
